text: Laparoscopic Keyhole Modified Technique for Stomal Prolapse Repair Colonic Stomal Prolapse is a common stoma-related complication, affecting the majority of patients with a stoma, especially those with end colostomy. In this video we present the case of a seventy-one year old male with a history of chondrosarcoma and several surgeries, a permanent sacral plexus lesion with fecal incontinence and neurogenic bladder. He had a stomal prolapse a few months after a Hartmann procedure performed one year earlier for perforated diverticulitis. The prolapse is moderate when the patient is laying down, but becomes incapacitating when the patient is standing or sitting, with pain and discomfort. The patient received a Stomach Quality of Life questionnaire and he reported a significant impairment due to the severe prolapse. He was on chronic pain medication for his chondrosarcoma, therefore we could not use any questionnaire on pain score, cause it would be biased by the therapy.

The surgeon performs a combined operation using a keyhole technique followed by a conduit creation without mesh. After establishing pneumoperitoneum on the right flank, two trochars are inserted: a 5mm trochar in the right iliac fossa and a 10mm trochar at the epigastrium. Adhesions are freed circumferentially. A DynaMesh IPOM mesh is cut and inserted through the 10mm trochar, surrounding the bowel. The mesh is fixed to the abdominal wall with absorbable and non-absorbable tacks, and its cut margins overlap and are closed with stitches, wrapping the bowel as it exits the abdominal cavity.

Compared to ePTFE-derived meshes which lose up to fifty percent of their original size, the PVDF-derived meshes as DynaMesh lose less than twenty percent of their original size, with a long-term stability even better than polypropylene meshes. With the keyhole method, if the mesh shrinks, then the slit tends to widen. This combined with the forces from the intra-abdominal pressure may lead to recurrence. Instead of using a second mesh to cover the bowel approximated to the abdominal wall, as in the so-called sandwich technique, we decided to create a ten to twelve centimeter route for the end column with interruptive sutures. A few stitches PDS-polydeoxanon-trio on each side fix the edges of the serosa of the bowel to the abdominal wall to reduce the risk of prolapse hernia over time.

The recovery was uneventful and the patient was discharged home on post-op day four. The abdominal CT scan at three and six month follow-up confirmed the good results. The stomach quality of life questionnaire revealed a significant score improvement.

The well-known surgical options to repair a stomal prolapse and a parastomal hernia, keyhole, sugar baker and so-called hamburger or burger techniques will remain certainly the standard. However, we are just confident that this modified technique can be a safe and effective option to be offered to these patients. In the last three years, we have operated on four patients with parastomal hernia and or stomal prolapse, and we have obtained good results in three of these patients, at an average follow-up of two years. We certainly know that we need more time and a larger case series to prove we can rely on this modified procedure.
